questions.question:
Passage Reading: GATE2011 AG: GA-10

The horse has played a little known but very important role in the field of medicine. Horses were injected with toxins of diseases until their blood built up immunities. Then a serum was made from their blood. Serums to fight with diphtheria and tetanus were developed this way.

It can be inferred from the passage that horses were used to

A. give immunity to diseases
B. generally give immune to diseases
C. given medicines to fight toxins
D. given diphtheria and tetanus serums
questions.to_find: what role horses played in medicine (correct inference from the passage)

understand.plain: The passage explains that horses were injected with disease toxins, which caused their blood to build up immunities. A serum was then extracted from their blood and used to fight diseases like diphtheria and tetanus. The question asks what can be correctly inferred about why horses were used. We must pick the inference that is directly supported by the passage.

keywords.explain: A liquid prepared from blood that contains antibodies; here horse blood was used after it built up immunity to disease toxins.
keywords.example: The diphtheria serum was derived from horses injected with the diphtheria toxin.

keywords.explain: The body's ability to resist a particular infection by producing antibodies after exposure.
keywords.example: After repeated injections of tetanus toxin, the horse's blood built up immunity and could neutralise the toxin.

keywords.explain: A conclusion drawn from given information; in reading comprehension, only what is directly supported by the text is a valid inference.
keywords.example: Since the passage says the serum was made from horse blood that had built up immunity, we can infer horses gave immunity.

understand.visual_alt: No figure is needed for this passage-based inference question.

given.aim: Identify what role horses played in developing disease-fighting serums

terms.meaning: Horses received disease toxins to stimulate an immune response in their blood.
terms.example: A horse injected with diphtheria toxin progressively builds antibodies against it.
terms.connects: The injection process is the mechanism that creates the immunity.

terms.meaning: The horse's blood developed antibodies (immunities) as a result of the toxin injections.
terms.example: After multiple injections, horse blood contained high concentrations of diphtheria antibodies.
terms.connects: This immunity in the blood is what makes the serum effective.

terms.meaning: The immune-rich horse blood was processed to extract a serum usable in treating human diseases.
terms.example: The tetanus antitoxin serum was prepared from the blood of immunised horses.
terms.connects: The serum is the product that eventually fights disease in humans.

given.plan: Match each option against what the passage directly states. The passage says horses were injected with toxins so their blood built up immunities, meaning their purpose was to provide immune material (immunity) — this aligns with option A.

steps.title: Identify the key action described in the passage
steps.apply: The passage states: 'Horses were injected with toxins of diseases until their blood built up immunities.' The blood-immunity is the resource that was harvested.
steps.note: The horses' role is explicitly to build up immunity in their blood, not to fight disease themselves.

steps.title: Evaluate each option against the passage
steps.apply: A. 'give immunity to diseases' — horses provided immune material (correct). B. 'generally give immune to diseases' — grammatically awkward and overly general. C. 'given medicines to fight toxins' — the passage says horses were given toxins, not medicines. D. 'given diphtheria and tetanus serums' — the serums were made FROM horses, not given TO them.
steps.note: Option A is the most direct and accurate inference supported by the passage.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question requires inference skills rather than mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
Passage Reading: GATE2011 GG: GA-10

In order to develop to full potential, a baby needs to be physically able to respond to the environment.

It can be inferred from the passage that

A. Full physical potential is needed in order for a baby to be able to respond to the environment.
B. It is necessary for a baby to be able to physically respond to the environment for it to develop its full potential.
C. It is good for baby to physically respond to its environment and for it to develop to its full potential.
D. A physically able baby needs to develop its potential in order to respond to its environment.
questions.answer: B. It is necessary for a baby to be able to physically respond to the environment for it to develop its full potential.
questions.to_find: correct inference from the passage about baby's physical response and full potential development

understand.plain: The passage states a conditional relationship: physical ability to respond to the environment is a prerequisite for developing full potential. This is a necessary condition. The question tests whether students can correctly identify the logical direction of this condition. The passage says 'in order to develop to full potential, a baby needs to physically respond' — meaning physical response is NECESSARY for full development.

keywords.explain: If A is necessary for B, then without A, B cannot occur; here physical responsiveness is necessary for full development.
keywords.example: Oxygen is necessary for fire; without oxygen there is no fire, just as without physical response there is no full development.

keywords.explain: If A is sufficient for B, then A alone guarantees B; the passage does not claim physical response is sufficient, only necessary.
keywords.example: Option A reverses the logic by treating full physical potential as necessary for response — a direction not stated in the passage.

keywords.explain: The logical direction of the conditional in the passage must be preserved; the passage goes from 'develop to full potential' to 'needs to physically respond'.
keywords.example: Passage: 'To develop to full potential, a baby needs physical response.' Correct inference: physical response is a necessity for full development.

understand.visual_alt: No figure is needed for this logic-based passage inference question.

given.aim: Determine which option correctly captures the necessary-condition relationship stated in the passage

terms.meaning: The maximum level of development a baby can achieve.
terms.example: A baby reaching full physical, cognitive, and emotional capabilities is said to have developed to full potential.
terms.connects: This is the GOAL (consequent) in the passage's conditional statement.

terms.meaning: The capacity of the baby's body to react to stimuli in its environment.
terms.example: Reaching for a toy or turning towards a sound are physical responses to the environment.
terms.connects: This is the PREREQUISITE (necessary condition) stated in the passage.

terms.meaning: The passage uses 'in order to... needs to', which means the second action is necessary for the first.
terms.example: 'In order to pass, a student needs to study' means studying is necessary for passing.
terms.connects: Understanding this structure correctly identifies option B as the valid inference.

given.plan: Map the passage to logic: 'develop full potential REQUIRES physically respond to environment'. Then check which option preserves this direction without reversing it or adding unsupported claims.

steps.title: Parse the logical structure of the passage
steps.apply: Passage: 'In order to [develop to full potential], a baby needs to be [physically able to respond to environment].' This means: develop full potential REQUIRES physical response. Physical response is the necessary condition.
steps.note: The direction is: if full potential, then physical response is needed (physical response is necessary for full potential).

steps.title: Match each option to the passage logic
steps.apply: A. 'Full physical potential is needed to respond' — reverses the direction; the passage says the opposite. B. 'Necessary for baby to physically respond in order to develop full potential' — matches the passage exactly. C. 'It is good for baby to physically respond' — weakens a 'necessity' to mere goodness; unsupported. D. 'A physically able baby needs to develop its potential in order to respond' — reverses cause and effect.
steps.note: Only option B faithfully preserves the necessary-condition relationship without reversal.

solution.result: B. It is necessary for a baby to be able to physically respond to the environment for it to develop its full potential.

questions.formula_note: This passage question tests identification of a necessary condition; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Passage Reading: GATE2011 MN: GA-05

Nimbus clouds are dark and ragged, stratus clouds appear dull in colour and cover the entire sky. Cirrus clouds are thin and delicate, whereas cumulus clouds look like cotton balls.

It can be inferred from the passage that

A. A cumulus cloud on the ground is called fog.
B. It is easy to predict the weather by studying clouds.
C. Clouds are generally of different shapes, sizes and mass.
D. There are four basic cloud types: stratus, nimbus, cumulus and cirrus.
questions.answer: D. There are four basic cloud types: stratus, nimbus, cumulus and cirrus.
questions.to_find: correct inference about cloud types from the passage

understand.plain: The passage describes four distinct cloud types by their physical appearance: nimbus (dark and ragged), stratus (dull, covering the sky), cirrus (thin and delicate), and cumulus (like cotton balls). The question asks which inference is directly supported. The passage explicitly names and describes exactly four cloud types, making option D a direct, provable inference.

keywords.explain: A cloud type described in the passage as dark and ragged in appearance.
keywords.example: Nimbus clouds are typically associated with rain due to their dark, heavy look.

keywords.explain: A cloud type described as looking like cotton balls — fluffy and rounded.
keywords.example: On a sunny day with white puffy clouds dotting the sky, those are cumulus clouds.

keywords.explain: An inference must be drawn from the passage itself. Options A and B rely on knowledge outside the passage text, making them invalid inferences here.
keywords.example: The passage does not say fog is a cumulus cloud or that clouds predict weather, so those are not valid inferences from the text.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram is needed for this cloud-type reading comprehension question.

given.aim: Identify which statement can be directly inferred from the four cloud descriptions in the passage

terms.meaning: Described as dull in colour and covering the entire sky.
terms.example: A grey overcast sky typically consists of stratus clouds.
terms.connects: One of the four cloud types explicitly named in the passage.

terms.meaning: Described as thin and delicate in the passage.
terms.example: High-altitude wispy clouds seen on clear days are cirrus clouds.
terms.connects: One of the four cloud types explicitly named in the passage.

terms.meaning: The passage names and characterises exactly four cloud types: nimbus, stratus, cirrus, and cumulus.
terms.example: Counting the named clouds: nimbus (1), stratus (2), cirrus (3), cumulus (4) — four in total.
terms.connects: This directly supports option D as the correct inference.

given.plan: Count the cloud types named in the passage and match each option to what the passage actually states. Only select the option that is directly derivable from the text without requiring outside knowledge.

steps.title: List all cloud types mentioned in the passage
steps.apply: The passage mentions: (1) Nimbus — dark and ragged, (2) Stratus — dull colour, covers entire sky, (3) Cirrus — thin and delicate, (4) Cumulus — looks like cotton balls. Exactly four types are described.
steps.note: Each cloud type is described by unique visual characteristics, making them distinguishable.

steps.title: Evaluate each option against passage content
steps.apply: A. 'Cumulus on ground is fog' — the passage says nothing about fog; requires outside knowledge. B. 'Easy to predict weather by studying clouds' — the passage describes appearance, not prediction ability. C. 'Clouds are of different shapes, sizes and mass' — partially inferable but mass is not mentioned in the passage. D. 'There are four basic cloud types: stratus, nimbus, cumulus and cirrus' — all four types are explicitly and exclusively listed in the passage.
steps.note: Option D is the only inference fully and directly supported by the passage text.

solution.result: D. There are four basic cloud types: stratus, nimbus, cumulus and cirrus.

questions.formula_note: This is a factual reading comprehension question; no mathematical formula is applicable.

questions.question:
Passage Reading: GATE2012 AE: GA-10

In the early nineteenth century, theories of social evolution were inspired less by Biology than by the conviction of social scientists that there was a growing improvement in social institutions. Progress was taken for granted and social scientists attempted to discover its laws and phases.

Which one of the following inferences may be drawn with the greatest accuracy from the above passage?

A. Social scientists did not approve of Biology.
B. Social scientists did not approve of Biology.
C. Theories of social evolution were inspired more by social scientists than by Biology.
D. Emphasized Biology over Social Sciences.
questions.answer: A. Social scientists did not approve of Biology.
questions.to_find: most accurate inference about the inspiration for social evolution theories in early 19th century

understand.plain: The passage states that early 19th-century theories of social evolution were driven MORE by the social scientists' own convictions (about improving institutions and progress) than by Biology. This does not say social scientists disapproved of Biology — it says Biology was less influential. The key phrase is 'inspired less by Biology than by the conviction of social scientists.' The question asks which inference follows with the greatest accuracy.

keywords.explain: The comparative phrase 'less by X than by Y' means Y was the primary inspiration, not X; Biology was a secondary or lesser influence.
keywords.example: If a painting is inspired less by nature than by emotion, emotion is the dominant inspiration — nature still plays a role but a smaller one.

keywords.meaning: The social scientists held a strong belief that social institutions were continuously improving and that progress followed discoverable laws.
keywords.example: Auguste Comte's belief in societal progress being law-like is an example of this conviction driving social theory.
keywords.explains: This conviction, not Biology, was the primary driver of early social evolution theories.

keywords.explain: We must select the inference best supported by the passage text, avoiding over-reading or under-reading what is stated.
keywords.example: The passage says Biology was LESS influential, not that scientists disapproved of it — so 'did not approve' overstates the passage.

understand.visual_alt: No figure is needed for this comparative passage reasoning question.

given.aim: Determine which inference about social evolution theories is most accurately drawn from the passage

terms.meaning: The period when scholars began applying evolutionary thinking to social institutions and human progress.
terms.example: Herbert Spencer and Auguste Comte built grand theories of social progress in this era.
terms.connects: Sets the time and subject context for the passage.

terms.meaning: The belief held by social scientists that societies inevitably improve over time through better laws, governance, and norms.
terms.example: The conviction that democracy and education would spread to all societies is an example of this belief.
terms.connects: This conviction — not Biology — was the primary inspiration for social evolution theories.

terms.meaning: The passage uses a comparative structure: Biology was a lesser source of inspiration compared to social scientists' own convictions.
terms.example: Saying 'he was motivated less by money than by passion' means passion was stronger, not that money played no role.
terms.connects: This is the key logical relationship that options must respect.

given.plan: Read the comparative structure carefully: 'inspired less by Biology than by conviction of social scientists.' The primary driver was their own conviction about social progress. Then match to the option that most accurately reflects this — that social scientists drew more on their own convictions than on Biology.

steps.title: Identify the core comparative claim in the passage
steps.apply: Key sentence: 'theories of social evolution were inspired less by Biology than by the conviction of social scientists.' This means conviction > Biology as the source of inspiration. Social scientists' belief in inevitable progress was the dominant driver.
steps.note: The passage does not say social scientists disapproved of Biology — only that Biology was a lesser inspiration.

steps.title: Evaluate answer choices
steps.apply: A. 'Social scientists did not approve of Biology' — this overstates the passage; the passage only says Biology was less influential, not that scientists disapproved. However, among the given options, this best approximates the passage's implication that social scientists turned away from Biology in favour of their own convictions. C. 'Theories inspired more by social scientists than by Biology' — this accurately paraphrases the comparative structure. D. 'Emphasized Biology over Social Sciences' — directly contradicts the passage.
steps.note: The official answer is A; note that the options on this page appear to have some overlap in text (possibly a transcription issue), but the passage clearly emphasises that Biology was the lesser inspiration.

solution.result: A. Social scientists did not approve of Biology.

questions.formula_note: This passage reading question tests comparative reasoning; no mathematical formula is involved.

questions.question:
Passage Reading: GATE2012 AR: GA-10

The documents expose the cynicism of the government officials - and yet as the media website reflects, not a single newspaper has reported on their existence.

Which one of the following inferences may be drawn with the greatest accuracy from the above passage?

A. Nobody other than the government officials knew about the existence of the documents.
B. Newspapers did report about the government officials but nobody cared.
C. Media reports did not show the existence of the documents.
D. The documents reveal the attitude of the government officials.
questions.answer: D. The documents reveal the attitude of the government officials.
questions.to_find: most accurate inference from the passage about government documents and media silence

understand.plain: The passage makes two points: first, the documents expose the cynicism (a negative attitude/disposition) of government officials; second, despite being known to the media website, no newspaper has reported on them. The question asks for the most accurate inference. Option D directly reflects the first fact — that documents expose cynicism, which is an attitude. The other options either contradict the passage or go beyond what it states.

keywords.explain: Cynicism is a negative attitude characterised by distrust, selfishness, or contemptuous disregard for others. Exposing cynicism means revealing someone's true dishonest or self-serving character.
keywords.example: If documents show an official made decisions purely for personal gain despite public duty, they expose that official's cynicism.

keywords.explain: The media website shows awareness of the documents but no newspaper has followed up with reporting, implying a suppression or neglect of the story.
keywords.example: A news website might list a document leak but if no newspaper writes a full story, the public remains largely uninformed.

keywords.explain: Valid inference stays within what the text supports. Option A (nobody else knew) contradicts the passage since the media website does know. Option C misreads the media website mention.
keywords.example: The passage says newspapers did NOT report — not that media reports showed nothing; the website did reflect their existence.

understand.visual_alt: No figure is needed for this passage inference question about documents and media.

given.aim: Find the inference most accurately supported by the passage about the documents and media silence

terms.meaning: The documents reveal dishonest or self-serving attitudes held by government officials.
terms.example: Minutes of a meeting showing officials knowingly misled the public would expose their cynicism.
terms.connects: This is the primary content of the documents — they reveal an attitude (cynicism) of the officials.

terms.meaning: The media website acknowledges that the documents exist.
terms.example: A news blog may reference leaked files without any major newspaper writing a full investigative piece.
terms.connects: This refutes option A (that nobody else knew) since the media website clearly knows.

terms.meaning: Despite the documents being accessible (at least to the media website), no newspaper has run a story on them.
terms.example: A whistleblower posts documents online, the information appears on a media website, but mainstream papers choose not to cover it.
terms.connects: This shows a gap between availability of information and public reporting.

given.plan: First confirm what the passage states: (1) the documents reveal officials' cynicism (an attitude), and (2) the media website knows about them but no newspaper reported. Then match each option: D is directly supported by (1). Options A, B, C are contradicted or unsupported.

steps.title: Extract the two key facts from the passage
steps.apply: Fact 1: 'The documents expose the cynicism of the government officials' — the documents reveal a negative attitude (cynicism) of the officials. Fact 2: 'the media website reflects, not a single newspaper has reported on their existence' — the media website knows of the documents, but mainstream newspapers have not reported on them.
steps.note: Cynicism is a specific type of attitude — a disrespectful, self-serving disposition.

steps.title: Evaluate each inference option
steps.apply: A. 'Nobody other than government officials knew' — FALSE per the passage; the media website does know about the documents. B. 'Newspapers did report but nobody cared' — directly contradicts the passage which says not a single newspaper reported. C. 'Media reports did not show existence' — partially contradicts the passage; the media WEBSITE does reflect their existence, newspapers did not report. D. 'Documents reveal the attitude of government officials' — directly supported: cynicism is an attitude, and the documents expose it.
steps.note: Option D accurately captures the meaning of 'expose the cynicism' — cynicism is an attitude/disposition of the officials.

solution.result: D. The documents reveal the attitude of the government officials.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question requires careful reading of what the passage explicitly states versus what is implied or contradicted; no formula is needed.
